title: Ada and the Great Horse Migration
synopsis: In 1880s Montana, eight-year-old Ada discovers a lost mustang foal and must help reunite it with the wild herd before winter arrives. With the help of her diverse group of friends and their different cultural knowledge about horses, Ada learns about courage, friendship, and the importance of protecting wild animals.

chapters.title: The Lost Foal
chapters.content:
Ada Chen pressed her nose against the frosty window of the one-room schoolhouse. Outside, the Montana plains stretched endlessly under gray October clouds. She could barely pay attention to Miss Johnson's arithmetic lesson when the wild world was calling to her.

"Ada, can you tell us what seven times eight equals?" Miss Johnson asked.

Ada spun around, her long black braids swinging. "Um... fifty-six?"

"Correct. Now please pay attention."

Beside her, her best friend Miguel whispered, "You're so lucky you're good at numbers. I was totally lost."

Ada grinned at Miguel. His family had moved to their small town of Willowbrook from Mexico two years ago, and he always made her laugh with his jokes. On her other side sat Kenji, whose Japanese family ran the general store, and behind them was Amara, whose grandmother told the most amazing stories about life before the town existed.

When the school bell finally rang, Ada shot out of her seat like a prairie dog popping up from its burrow.

"Race you to the creek!" she called to her friends.

The four children ran across the golden grassland, their boots crunching on frost-covered ground. Ada loved this time of year when the cottonwood trees turned yellow and the air smelled like snow was coming.

But when they reached Willow Creek, Ada stopped short. There, standing alone by the water's edge, was the most beautiful sight she'd ever seen.

A baby horse – no bigger than a large dog – stood shivering in the cold. Its coat was the color of chocolate with a white star on its forehead. The foal's big brown eyes looked scared and lost.

"Oh my," breathed Amara. "It's just a baby."

"Where's its mama?" Miguel asked, looking around.

"I don't see any other horses," Kenji said quietly. "It must be lost."

Ada took a careful step forward. The foal lifted its head and looked right at her. Something in those dark eyes made her heart squeeze tight.

"Hey there, little one," she whispered. "It's okay. We won't hurt you."

The foal took a tiny step toward her, then stopped.

"My grandmother always said wild horses are sacred," Amara said softly. "They've been running free on these plains for hundreds of years."

"But this one's too little to be alone," Ada said. "What if it gets hurt? What if wolves find it?"

Miguel nodded. "My papa says wolves come down from the mountains when it gets cold."

Ada made up her mind. "We have to help it find its family."

"But how?" Kenji asked. "We don't know where the wild horses are."

Ada thought hard. Her father had taught her to solve problems step by step, the same way he solved problems with the railroad he helped build.

"First, we need to make sure it's safe tonight," she said. "Then tomorrow we'll figure out how to find the herd."

The foal whinnied softly, as if it understood.

illustrations.description: Four diverse children standing by a creek in 1880s Montana, looking at a small chocolate-colored foal with a white star. Ada has long black braids and determined eyes, Miguel wears a wool jacket, Kenji has neat clothing from his merchant family, and Amara has warm brown skin and traditional braided hair. Golden autumn light filters through cottonwood trees, casting long shadows on frost-covered grass. The foal looks vulnerable but hopeful.
illustrations.alt: Ada and her three friends discovering the lost foal by Willow Creek

chapters.title: A Plan Forms
chapters.content:
That evening, Ada could barely eat her mother's steamed fish and vegetables. All she could think about was the little foal, alone and cold by the creek.

"Ada, you're pushing your food around like you're building a railroad," her father said with a gentle smile. "What's troubling you?"

Ada looked at her parents. Her father worked as a translator for the railroad company, helping Chinese workers communicate with the bosses. Her mother ran a small laundry business. They were practical people who solved problems.

"Papa, what would you do if you found a baby animal that was lost from its family?"

Her mother set down her chopsticks. "That's a big responsibility, Ada. Wild animals belong with their own kind."

"But what if the baby might die without help?" Ada pressed.

Her father was quiet for a moment. "Sometimes, little daughter, we must take risks to do what's right. But we must be very careful and ask for help from people who know more than we do."

After dinner, Ada ran to Miguel's house, then to Kenji's, then to Amara's. Soon all four friends were gathered in Ada's family's small parlor.

"My papa says there's a wild horse herd that comes through every fall," Miguel reported. "They're heading south for the winter. But he doesn't know exactly where."

"Mr. Thompson at the general store might know," Kenji suggested. "He talks to all the ranchers."

Amara shook her head. "My grandmother says some of the white ranchers don't like the wild horses. They think they eat too much grass that cattle need."

"But the horses were here first," Ada said firmly.

"I know," Amara agreed. "But not everyone sees it that way."

Ada's mind raced. "Okay, so we need to find someone who knows about horses AND cares about keeping them safe."

"What about Old Pete?" Miguel suggested. "He used to be a cowboy before he got too old. He lives in that little cabin near the ridge."

Kenji nodded. "He's always been nice to me and my family. And he knows everything about animals."

"That's perfect!" Ada jumped up. "Tomorrow after school, we'll go ask him. But first, we need to check on the foal."

"What if it's gone?" Amara asked worriedly.

"Then we'll look for tracks," Ada said. "Papa taught me how to read signs on the ground from his railroad work."

Miguel grinned. "You always have a plan, Ada."

"That's what friends are for," she replied. "We help each other figure things out."

As her friends headed home, Ada looked out at the starry sky. Somewhere out there, a little foal was spending its first night alone. She just hoped it would be safe until morning.

"Please be okay, little star," she whispered, thinking of the white marking on the foal's forehead. "We're going to help you find your family."

illustrations.description: Interior of Ada's 1880s home showing a cozy parlor with oil lamps casting warm golden light. Four children sit in a circle planning - Ada gesturing excitedly, Miguel leaning forward, Kenji sitting thoughtfully, and Amara listening intently. Chinese decorative elements blend with frontier furniture. Through the window, stars twinkle in the dark Montana sky. The scene conveys determination and friendship.
illustrations.alt: Ada and friends planning in her family's parlor by lamplight

chapters.title: Old Pete's Wisdom
chapters.content:
The next morning, Ada could hardly sit still during lessons. When Miss Johnson asked her to recite the state capitals, Ada's mind was on horses instead of geography.

"Ada, where is the capital of Colorado?" Miss Johnson repeated.

"Um... Denver?" Ada guessed, earning a nod from her teacher.

After what felt like the longest school day ever, the four friends raced to Willow Creek. Ada's heart pounded as they approached the spot where they'd seen the foal.

"There!" Amara pointed.

The little foal was still there, but it looked thinner and sadder. It had found some shelter under a fallen log, but Ada could see it was shivering.

"Poor little guy," Miguel said softly.

Ada approached slowly and sat down a few feet away from the foal. "We're going to help you," she promised.

Then they set off for Old Pete's cabin. The grizzled cowboy was sitting on his porch, whittling a piece of wood. His weathered face broke into a smile when he saw them coming.

"Well, if it ain't my favorite young'uns," he said in his gravelly voice. "What brings you all the way out here?"

"Mr. Pete, we found a baby horse that's lost," Ada said breathlessly. "Can you help us?"

Old Pete's expression grew serious. "A foal, you say? This time of year? That's mighty troublesome."

He listened carefully as they described the little horse and where they'd found it.

"Sounds like one of the mustangs from the Thunder Ridge herd," he said thoughtfully. "They usually pass through this area in October, heading south for winter."

"Do you know where they are now?" Kenji asked.

"Well, I heard tell they were spotted near Devil's Canyon yesterday. That's about ten miles north of here." Old Pete scratched his gray beard. "Question is, how'd a little one get separated?"

"Maybe it got scared and ran the wrong way?" Ada suggested.

"Could be. Could be." Old Pete stood up. "Tell you what, kids. Tomorrow's Saturday. If'n your folks say it's okay, I'll saddle up Buttercup and we'll ride out to find that herd."

"Really?" all four children said together.

Old Pete chuckled. "Can't leave a baby out there alone. 'Sides, I know the lead mare of that herd. Beautiful black horse named Thunder. She's smart as a whip and tough as nails. If any horse can keep a herd safe through winter, it's her."

"What about the foal tonight?" Amara asked.

"We'll keep watch," Old Pete said. "I'll bring some hay and a blanket. Wild horses don't usually take to human help, but babies are different. Sometimes they know when they need us."

As they walked back toward town, Ada felt hope bubbling up inside her like a spring.

"We're really going to reunite the foal with its family," she said.

"If we can find them," Miguel added.

"We will," Ada said confidently. "I just know it."

illustrations.description: Old Pete's rustic log cabin with a covered porch, surrounded by golden aspen trees on a Montana hillside. The elderly cowboy sits whittling while four children gather around him eagerly. Pete has kind gray eyes and weathered hands, wearing worn leather and denim. His faithful horse Buttercup grazes nearby. Afternoon sunlight creates long shadows, and mountains rise in the distance. The scene feels peaceful and hopeful.
illustrations.alt: Old Pete on his cabin porch talking with the children about the wild horses

chapters.title: The Search Begins
chapters.content:
Saturday morning dawned crisp and clear. Ada had barely slept, dreaming of wild horses running across endless prairies. She gulped down her breakfast and ran to meet her friends.

All four sets of parents had agreed to let their children go with Old Pete, as long as they stayed together and were back before dark.

"Remember, Ada," her mother said, "wild animals can be dangerous. Listen to Mr. Pete and be very careful."

"I will, Mama," Ada promised.

They met Old Pete at the creek where the foal was waiting. The little horse seemed to recognize them now and didn't back away when they approached.

"Well, I'll be," Old Pete said softly. "Look at that. Little fella's decided to trust you."

Ada knelt down and slowly extended her hand. The foal sniffed her fingers, then gently nuzzled her palm.

"His nose is so soft," she whispered.

"That's a good sign," Old Pete said. "Means he's not giving up hope."

Old Pete had brought his gentle mare Buttercup, along with a long lead rope. "We'll see if we can get the little one to follow us partway. Then we'll have to carry him if needed."

To everyone's surprise, when they started walking north toward Devil's Canyon, the foal followed them! He stayed close to Ada, as if she was his new big sister.

"Look at that," Miguel marveled. "He really likes you, Ada."

"Animals can sense kindness," Amara said. "My grandmother always told me that."

As they walked, Old Pete taught them about reading the landscape.

"See those tracks there?" He pointed to marks in the soft earth near a stream. "That's where horses stopped to drink. And look at those trees – bark's been rubbed off about chest-high. That's from horses scratching their backs."

Kenji studied the signs carefully. "It's like reading a story written in the ground."

"Exactly right, son," Old Pete said approvingly.

They'd been walking for about two hours when Old Pete suddenly held up his hand.

"Hold up," he whispered. "You hear that?"

Everyone stopped and listened. In the distance, Ada could hear a sound that made her heart race – the thunder of many hooves running across the prairie.

"Is that them?" she whispered.

Old Pete's eyes crinkled with excitement. "That's them, all right. Now comes the tricky part."

The foal suddenly lifted its head and whinnied loudly. From somewhere ahead came an answering call – deep and powerful.

"That's Thunder herself," Old Pete said. "She's calling for her babies."

The little foal began prancing excitedly, pulling toward the sound.

"He knows his mama's voice!" Ada said, tears of joy springing to her eyes.

"Come on," Old Pete said. "Let's go reunite this family."

illustrations.description: A sweeping Montana prairie landscape with Old Pete leading his horse Buttercup while four children walk alongside. The small chocolate foal stays close to Ada, who reaches out protectively. Golden morning light illuminates the vast grassland dotted with sagebrush and wildflowers. In the far distance, dust clouds hint at the approaching wild horse herd. The scene conveys adventure and anticipation, with big sky and rolling hills stretching to snow-capped mountains.
illustrations.alt: The group walking across the prairie with the foal following Ada

chapters.title: Thunder and Her Herd
chapters.content:
As they crested a small hill, Ada gasped. Below them in a wide valley, at least thirty wild horses grazed and moved restlessly. They were every color imaginable – black, brown, white, spotted, and golden. But the most magnificent was the horse Old Pete had called Thunder.

She was coal black with a flowing mane and tail, and she stood taller than all the others. Her head was raised high, nostrils flaring as she tested the wind.

"She's beautiful," Amara whispered.

"And smart," Old Pete added. "She knows we're here. Question is, will she let us approach?"

The little foal beside Ada began whinnying frantically. Several horses in the herd lifted their heads and called back.

"There!" Miguel pointed. "That brown mare – she's coming this way!"

A horse the same chocolate color as the foal was pushing through the herd, nickering urgently. When she saw the foal, she trumpeted with joy.

"That's his mama!" Ada exclaimed.

But Thunder stepped between the approaching mare and the humans. The big black horse's ears were pinned back, and she pawed the ground warningly.

"She's protecting her herd," Old Pete explained. "Can't blame her. Humans haven't always been kind to wild horses."

Ada's heart sank. They'd come so far, but now Thunder wouldn't let the foal return to his family.

Then Amara stepped forward. She began humming something low and melodic – a song her grandmother had taught her.

To everyone's amazement, Thunder's ears pricked forward. The big mare tilted her head, listening.

"Keep singing," Old Pete whispered. "I think she likes it."

Amara's voice grew stronger, singing words in her grandmother's language. It was a song about the bond between all living things, about respect and harmony.

Slowly, Thunder took a step forward. Then another. The powerful horse approached until she was just ten feet away from the children.

Ada held her breath. Thunder was even more beautiful up close. Her dark eyes seemed to look right into Ada's soul.

Then something magical happened. The little foal walked right past Thunder to his mother. The brown mare nuzzled her baby all over, whickering with relief and joy.

"She's letting him go home," Kenji said softly.

Thunder watched for a moment, then approached Ada. The great horse lowered her magnificent head and breathed softly on Ada's face.

"She's thanking you," Old Pete said quietly. "She knows you helped her family."

Tears rolled down Ada's cheeks as she looked into Thunder's wise eyes. "Take good care of him," she whispered.

Thunder snorted gently, as if she understood. Then she turned and trotted back to her herd, where the foal was busy nursing from his relieved mother.

"Will we ever see them again?" Ada asked.

"Maybe," Old Pete said. "Wild horses have long memories. They don't forget kindness."

illustrations.description: A dramatic valley scene with a wild horse herd spread across golden grassland. Thunder, a magnificent black mare, stands protectively in the foreground while a chocolate-colored mare reunites with her small foal. Ada and her friends stand respectfully at the valley's edge with Old Pete and Buttercup. Amara is singing, her arms slightly raised. The late afternoon sun casts everything in warm golden light, creating a sense of magic and connection between humans and wild horses.
illustrations.alt: Thunder's herd in the valley with the foal reuniting with its mother

chapters.title: Saying Goodbye
chapters.content:
The children stood quietly watching as the wild herd began to move. Thunder led the way, her black coat gleaming in the afternoon sun. The foal stayed close to his mother, occasionally looking back at Ada and her friends.

"They're heading south for winter," Old Pete explained. "They'll find sheltered valleys where the snow isn't as deep and there's still grass to eat."

"How far will they go?" Miguel asked.

"Maybe a hundred miles or more," Old Pete replied. "Wild horses are tough. They know how to survive."

As the herd moved away, Ada felt both happy and sad. Happy that the little foal was back with his family, but sad to see them go.

"I'm going to miss him," she said softly.

"Me too," Amara agreed. "But he belongs with them."

Kenji nodded thoughtfully. "My father always says that helping others sometimes means letting them go."

"That's wise words, son," Old Pete said.

The last horse was just disappearing over a distant ridge when Ada made a decision.

"We should tell everyone in town about what we saw today," she said. "About how beautiful and smart the wild horses are."

"Why?" Miguel asked.

"Because Amara said some people don't like the wild horses. But if they could see what we saw – how Thunder protected her family, how much the mother loved her baby – maybe they'd understand that wild horses deserve to stay free."

Old Pete smiled broadly. "Now that's good thinking, Miss Ada. Education's the best way to change minds."

On the walk back to town, they made plans. They would ask Miss Johnson if they could give a presentation at school about wild horses. Kenji would draw pictures for them to show. Amara would share her grandmother's stories about the horses' history on the plains.

"And I'll tell everyone about how smart and gentle they are," Miguel added.

"What will you do, Ada?" Amara asked.

Ada thought for a moment. "I'll tell them about courage. About how Thunder was brave enough to trust us, and how the little foal was brave enough to follow us to find his family."

As they reached the edge of Willowbrook, the sun was setting behind them, painting the sky pink and orange.

"Thank you, Mr. Pete," Ada said. "Today was the most amazing day of my life."

"Mine too," said Miguel.

"And mine," added Kenji and Amara together.

Old Pete tipped his hat. "Thank you, young'uns, for reminding an old cowboy why these horses are so special. They're not just animals – they're part of what makes this land wild and free."

That night, Ada dreamed of running across endless prairies with Thunder's herd, her hair streaming behind her like a horse's mane, feeling as free as the wind itself.

illustrations.description: A sunset scene showing the wild horse herd as distant silhouettes moving across rolling hills toward the horizon. Thunder leads the procession, with the foal and its mother visible among the group. In the foreground, Ada and her friends stand with Old Pete and Buttercup, waving goodbye. The sky blazes with pink and orange clouds, and the grassland glows golden in the fading light. The mood is bittersweet but hopeful, capturing both the beauty of goodbye and the joy of a successful rescue.
illustrations.alt: The children waving goodbye to Thunder's herd as they head south

chapters.title: Wild Hearts, Free Spirits
chapters.content:
Three weeks later, Ada stood nervously at the front of the packed schoolhouse. It seemed like half the town had come to hear their presentation about wild horses. Her parents sat in the front row, smiling encouragingly. Old Pete was there too, his best hat in his hands.

"Good evening, everyone," Ada began, her voice shaking slightly. "My friends and I want to tell you about the most amazing animals in Montana – our wild horses."

Kenji held up his beautiful drawings of Thunder and the herd. The audience murmured appreciatively at his artistic skills.

Amara told ancient stories her grandmother had shared about the first horses on the plains, and how Native peoples had lived in harmony with them for generations.

Miguel made everyone laugh with his dramatic retelling of their search for the herd, complete with sound effects of whinnying horses.

Then it was Ada's turn again. She looked out at all the faces – some curious, some skeptical, some already convinced.

"The little foal we found taught me something important," she said, her voice growing stronger. "Sometimes being brave means trusting others to help you. And sometimes being brave means helping others, even when it's hard."

She told them about Thunder's intelligence and protective instincts, about the mother's joy at finding her baby, about the way the whole herd worked together.

"Wild horses aren't just pretty animals," Ada concluded. "They're families. They take care of each other, just like we do. They deserve to stay free and wild, because that's what makes them special."

When she finished, the whole room erupted in applause. Mr. Henderson, one of the local ranchers, stood up.

"Miss Ada, you've given me a lot to think about," he said. "I always saw wild horses as competition for my cattle. But hearing about their family bonds... well, maybe there's room on this land for all of us."

Other townspeople nodded in agreement. Mrs. Patterson, who ran the boarding house, wiped tears from her eyes.

"That was beautiful, children. You've opened our hearts to see these magnificent creatures in a new way."

After everyone had gone home, Ada and her friends sat on the schoolhouse steps, looking up at the star-filled sky.

"Do you think we really changed some minds tonight?" Miguel asked.

"I think so," Amara said. "My grandmother always says one story can plant seeds in many hearts."

"I hope Thunder and the little foal are safe and warm somewhere," Kenji added.

Ada smiled, thinking of the powerful black mare and the chocolate-colored foal who had brought them all together on this adventure.

"Wherever they are, they're free," she said. "And that's what matters most."

Just then, carried on the cold night wind, they heard a sound that made them all sit up straight – the distant call of wild horses, free and running under the Montana stars.

"They're out there," Ada whispered. "Still wild, still free."

And in her heart, she knew that was exactly how it should be.

illustrations.description: Interior of a packed 1880s schoolhouse at evening, lit by warm oil lamps and lanterns. Ada stands confidently at the front presenting to a diverse audience of townspeople sitting on wooden benches. Kenji holds up detailed horse drawings, while Miguel, Amara, and Old Pete beam proudly from the audience. People of different backgrounds and ages listen attentively - ranchers, shopkeepers, families with children. Through the windows, stars twinkle in the dark sky. The warm golden light creates an atmosphere of community and shared understanding.
illustrations.alt: Ada presenting to the townspeople in the crowded schoolhouse

metadata.illustrationStyle: Warm, inviting adventure illustration style. Earthy tones, golden light. Detailed environments with hidden details kids can discover.

questions.question: What color was the lost foal that Ada and her friends found?
choices: Chocolate brown with a white star
questions.explanation: The foal was chocolate brown with a white star on its forehead, which is why Ada later called it 'little star.'

questions.question: Who was the leader of the wild horse herd?
questions.explanation: Thunder was the magnificent black mare who led the wild horse herd and protected her family.

questions.question: What did Amara do to help Thunder trust them?
questions.explanation: Amara sang a traditional song her grandmother had taught her about harmony between all living things, which helped Thunder feel more trusting.

questions.question: Why were the wild horses traveling south?
questions.explanation: The wild horses were migrating south for the winter to find sheltered valleys with less snow and available grass.

questions.question: What was Old Pete's horse's name?
questions.explanation: Old Pete's gentle mare was named Buttercup, and she helped them on their journey to find the wild horse herd.

questions.question: Where did Ada's father work?
questions.explanation: Ada's father worked as a translator for the railroad company, helping Chinese workers communicate with their bosses.

questions.question: What did the children decide to do after their adventure?
choices: Give a presentation to the town
questions.explanation: The children decided to educate their community by giving a presentation about wild horses to help people understand and appreciate them.

pages.title: Complete the Story
pages.instructions: Fill in the missing words to complete this passage from the story:
pages.text: Ada pressed her nose against the frosty _____ of the schoolhouse. Outside, the Montana _____ stretched endlessly under gray October clouds. When they reached Willow Creek, there stood a baby _____ no bigger than a large dog. Its coat was the color of _____ with a white _____ on its forehead.

pages.title: Wild Horse Facts
pages.instructions: Choose the correct words to complete these facts about wild horses:
pages.text: Thunder was a magnificent _____ mare who led the herd. The wild horses were traveling _____ for the winter. Old Pete said wild horses have long _____ and don't forget kindness. Amara's song helped Thunder learn to _____ the children.
